figures.caption: Figure 1: Overall architecture of MindAU. MindAU learns AU-discriminative EEG representations, aligns them with AU-level semantics and identity-reduced visual displacement trajectories, and conditions a multimodal diffusion editor for identity-preserving facial AU editing. MTP denotes the Manifold Trajectory Projector.
Figure 2: Visual comparison of our approach with other methods. The first, second, and third rows correspond to AU25 (Lips Part), AU9 (Nose Wrinkler), and AU12 (Lip Corner Puller).

Table 2: Ablation studies under the Self-Referenced and Cross-Referenced Editing protocols. “Direct” and “SupCon” denote pairwise cosine EEG–text alignment and supervised contrastive global-level alignment, respectively.
Figure 4: Qualitative results of Cross-Referenced Editing.
Figure 5: Qualitative comparison of the masking ablation under the self-reference setting.
Figure 6: Visualization of samples from the Self-Referenced Editing test subset.
Figure 7: Visualization of reference faces from a subset of the Cross-Referenced Editing test dataset.
Figure 8: Qualitative results of Self-Referenced Editing on the test dataset.
Figure 9: Qualitative results of Cross-Referenced Editing on the test dataset.
Figure 10: Visualization of E-token feature clustering extracted by the EEG adapter on the training set. Left: labeled by AU; Right: labeled by Subject.

Figure 11: Representative failure cases of MindAU. Some outputs exhibit incorrect AU generation or insufficient expression change, where the generated image remains overly close to the reference image.
Table 9: Existing assets used in this work and their licenses or terms of use.
